query: recent evidence on intraoperative neuromonitoring and functional outcomes in open TME

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Precentral Gyrus -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Tumors located within the primary motor cortex of the precentral gyrus are frequently considered unresectable due to the likelihood of creating new neurological deficits. However, the primary goal of glioma surgery is to achieve the maximal safe resection because the extent of this resection has been shown to increase survival for both low- and high-grade gliomas. New neurosurgical techniques have been introduced to achieve the maximal safe resection while decreasing the consequences seen postoperatively. One technique showing promise is the use of intraoperative stimulation mapping, which has been shown to decrease the incidence of severe neurological deficits by 60%. Even with new techniques, the risks of resecting gliomas in the precentral gyrus are still present. Up to 20% of patients who have undergone a resection involving the primary motor cortex or precentral gyrus have reported postoperative permanent motor deficits. The risk of a patient developing a motor deficit can still be up to 30% even with experienced hands, direct cortical stimulation, and intraoperative neuromonitoring. Given the potential for risks during precentral gyrus resections, patients should be counseled prior to surgery on the high probability of having a new or worsened motor dysfunction. However, these deficits usually partially or completely resolve with time. Several techniques have been found to help neurosurgeons perform safer resections. Surgical planning assistance is available with the use of preoperative transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), which is also useful to aid with functional MRI and diffusion tensor imaging (DTI)-based tractography and is beneficial when ruling out if there is functional involvement of the primary motor cortex. Using TMS may lead to greater gross-total glioma resection. Continuous motor mapping with direct cortical stimulation is being tried as a new technique to help make precentral gyrus resections safer. [10] [11] [12] [13] [12] [11] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative Neurophysiological Monitoring -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring. Describe the different techniques of intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring. Explain the importance of intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring to detect and prevent neurological injury. Review the importance of effective communication and close cooperation between interprofessional teams in providing high-quality peri-operative care to detect and prevent neurologic injuries. retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative Neurophysiological Monitoring -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The most commonly used intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring (IONM) techniques for surgical procedures include: Somatosensory sensory evoked potential (SSEP) Motor-evoked potential (MEP) Spontaneous and triggered electromyography (EMG) [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Sensory Nerves -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral nerve sheath tumors (PNST) can be benign or malignant. Benign tumors include neurofibromas or schwannomas, and malignant PNST are typically sarcomatous. Surgical consideration of the nerve fascicle that gave rise to the PNST involves intraoperative neurophysiologic monitoring to establish if the associated fascicle has a motor function because that determines whether to do a complete or partial resection. Malignant PNST involves wide local excision, sometimes consisting of proximal amputation. Studies have shown that pre-operative biopsy of PNST results in increased risk of postoperative neurologic deficit, suggesting that referral for surgery without biopsy is recommended. [20] [21] Finally, there is the management of nerve laceration, usually due to trauma. Sharp transection, such as seen with a stab wound, has a better prognosis than a crush injury. Repair involves surgical approximation of the damaged epineurium in a tension-free manner. For defects great than 5cm, the gold standard is autologous nerve grafting. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative Neurophysiological Monitoring -- Indications -- Electromyography (EMG)
retrieved_docs.content: To monitor cranial nerve function during procedures including acoustic neuroma resection, microvascular decompression of the facial nerve, parotid tumor resection, vestibular neurectomy for Meniere disease, neurotologic/otologic procedures. Nerve root or spinal cord monitoring during spinal surgeries including spinal instrumentation (e.g., pedicle screw placement), a mechanical spinal distraction Resection of skull base tumors, spinal tumors Surgical excision of cranial nerve neuromas of motor cranial nerve Brachial or lumbosacral plexus surgery Neck surgery including thyroid surgery, neck dissections

retrieved_docs.title: Intramedullary Spinal Cord Tumors -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical resection of intramedullary spinal cord tumors should be performed as soon as possible upon diagnosis due to the correlation between outcomes and the preoperative neurological condition, and observation can lead to further neurological deficits, some of which are irreversible. The primary objectives of surgical resection are to obtain tissue diagnosis, maximize safe tumor resection, and improve neurologic function. Intraoperative somatosensory and motor-evoked potential monitoring is used to detect any changes in neurological function and aid in resection. The extent of total resection greatly depends on the presence of a clear plane between the tumor and normal spinal cord tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative Neurophysiological Monitoring -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Different intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring techniques assess the function of the brain, brainstem, spinal cord, cranial nerves, and peripheral nerves during the procedure. They are immensely valuable in the detection and prevention of neurological insult. Intraoperative monitoring is now becoming part of standard medical practices and routinely used during many surgical procedures, including the risk of neurological injury. IONM employs a wide variety of physiological principles, each with a unique application and frequently used together in the same surgery, leading to improved patient outcomes. As the benefits of monitoring become apparent, the use of different neuromonitoring techniques during the additional surgical procedure has expanded. This activity reviews the different modalities of neurophysiological monitoring, their indications, and contraindications. This activity highlights the interprofessional team's role in evaluating and improving care for patients in providing high-quality peri-operative care to detect and prevent neurologic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative Neurophysiological Monitoring -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of development of neuro-deficit following spinal surgery with or without any recognizable adverse event is known. Intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring (IONM) use during spinal surgery, including motor-evoked potentials (MEPs), somatosensory-evoked potentials (SSEPs), and electromyography (EMG), leads to early recognition and management of any signal changes during the procedure, thus predicts a favorable surgical outcome. Loss of IONM signals or any variation from baseline IONM signal during surgery indicates a neural injury and predicts postoperative neuro deficits' development.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Awake Craniotomy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Awake craniotomy with brain mapping is the gold standard for resections of tumors or lesions in or near eloquent areas of the brain. Neurological deficits resulting from excision or injury of areas essential for speech and motor control functions might be devastating. The gold standard technique for mapping these vital areas involves direct electrical stimulation of the cortex while the patient is awake. The awake patients will be able to perform a relevant task and thus help determine if the stimulus disrupts the execution of the particular task.

retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative Neurophysiological Monitoring -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Enhancing communication between multidisciplinary teams increases the safety, quality, efficacy, and efficiency of perioperative care by decreasing adverse events and improving perioperative outcomes. [19] [Level 1] Various modalities of neurophysiological monitoring are used to monitor neural structures during different types of surgery. IONM team collaborates with the surgical and anesthesiology team to optimize signal acquisition and provide real-time analysis, interpretation, and timely communication of signal changes, which allows the surgeon to operate safely and avoid neural tissue injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative Neurophysiological Monitoring -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring (IONM) systems are equipped with all the channels to monitor different modalities of IONM needed for the specific type of surgery. The monitoring system displays continuous trends and raw signals, as well as it can also trigger the stimulation of specific muscle and nerve (peripheral, cranial) and records the signal. The system also records waveforms' time/date, detail waveform data, and the technologist's notes. The system usually has a provision for rejection of artifacts related to electrocautery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 622
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical action that functions as a parent category within the set of Clinical actions A‑G and is linked to answering specific clinical queries in the colorectal cancer diagnostic framework. It comprises numerous operative methods, including arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints, cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct (both with and without cholangiography), exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial regions, transcranial exploration of the orbit with lesion removal, lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, and parathyroidectomy with or without mediastinal exploration by a cervical approach. Excision is also identified as the method for other therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth and pharynx, as well as for percutaneous nephrolithotomy, and it has a relationship to plastic procedures on the nose. Certain spinal surgeries—laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two lumbar or thoracic vertebral segments (excluding spondylolisthesis)—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. This integrated representation clarifies which procedures are encompassed by Excision, which are excluded, and how the concept connects to broader clinical actions and diagnostic contexts, supporting precise procedural classification and patient‑centered care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad operative category in which direct visual or instrumental assessment of an anatomic region is performed, often together with therapeutic interventions, and are organized under the parent clinical‑action set “Clinical actions A‑G.” In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, dorsally, or via a lumbar approach, with or without biopsy—serves as an exploration procedure and may include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration comprises cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration and may be combined with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, followed by choledochotomy or choledochostomy for drainage, calculus removal, or cholecystotomy. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2879
retrieved_docs.content: Open prostatectomy is classified as an operation by approach, indicating that it is a surgical procedure performed through a direct, open incision to access the prostate. As a member of the broader category of operations by approach, open prostatectomy exemplifies surgeries that rely on an open technique rather than minimally invasive or endoscopic methods, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical strategies and informing patients about the nature of the procedure.
